speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I've got it here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you be able to reach it if you need it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I try to [? sit it, ?] because it's just broken, this chair. I don't think you can see.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: It's fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to take off the phone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, take off the phone. That would be good, yep. My name is Liora Miller. Today's date is the 14th of July, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Helena Rosner in Melbourne, Victoria, Australia. Can you tell me your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I am?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Helen Rosner.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, what-- is that your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: On birth, I was Haubenstock. But now I-- when I married my husband, I'm Rosner.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, did you have any nicknames when you were younger?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no-- well, nickname, what they call me at home, you mean? Oh, many. Helenka, [? Helusha, ?] [? Helushenka, ?] oh, many.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: 31st of July, '24.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what's your age now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will be 72 next, of this month, in July.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which country?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, in Poland, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your address prior to the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, we-- oh, before the wars we were in [POLISH] 12, [POLISH] in Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of house did you live in at that address?

speaker: interviewee
text: What-- well, I live a very happy life up there. I was young, and I was still going to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your house.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a flat. It wasn't a house, it was apartment. And I live in the second floor. And on the corner, corner was [POLISH] Street, and my father had a shop. And when I was going to school, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was living with you at-- in your flat?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my sister. My brother, not before the war, because my brother was in the army just before the war-- but my sister and my father, in [POLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother died before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did she die?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think in-- in '33 or '34, something like this. I was 11, I believe, when my mother died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: But that wasn't in [POLISH]. My mother died when we live in [POLISH]. That was a time of depression, in '30-- '35, '36, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that time. Where, where did you live then?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was outskirt of Cracow. It was cheaper out there, living. And because my father had problems with-- financial problem, that's why we change it from Cracow to [POLISH]-- [POLISH]. But you-- that was just before the war, you ask me. Well, that was before that, you know, that this was before that. That wasn't before the war. Before the war, [INAUDIBLE] I was without my mother. But through the war, long before this. You said after my mother-- my mother die in [POLISH]. It wasn't a telephone there, and it was no company up there. She was very lonely, my mother, and I was a little girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, in the house just before the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was [POLISH], yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about your school. Tell me about your school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went only to state school, you call it. And I was-- I started my school when I was seven years old. That was the-- I don't know why, but that's how I started. And I never went to any kindergarten. I never went to, to meet any other children, just right from home to school. And, well, it was a Catholic school, St. [POLISH], name. And was only three Jewish girl in the class. The whole school, maybe, have only 14 Jewish girls. It was a girls' school, very elegant. That was a nice school, because you know, Cracow got a part of Jewish suburb, Kazimierz. But we live just opposite, with the-- all Polish crowd around us. And I didn't know much about my Jewishness. [POLISH], they call me, [POLISH], which-- that mean little Jewish girl. They knew in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who called you that?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the girls or whoever, they wanted to please me. Instead of Jewish, they said [POLISH]. That means sweeter. And, but that was hurting me that time, when I was girl. It was hurting me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what hurt you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Especially when was before holiday, Christmas, or Easter, when we came to school-- when, when I started, first time I remember the girl start to cross themselves. And I didn't know what that mean, and I tried to just, just come-- do the same. And then I find out that I shouldn't do this, because I'm Jewish. But I knew all the prayers, because they pray every morning. And especially before Easter, when was this about crucified Jesus and Jesus. I remember one song, just from the back of Jesus, blood comes. Everybody looks at me, that I-- and I was-- because I was Jewish. I do remember this. It hurts, and I was a bit upset. But we have Jewish instruction once a month-- once a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the school, but in the different class. All these 14, or how many Jewish girl, it was a teacher. And his name was [POLISH]. I remember he was a little, and he liked girls. And I've been-- knew about Jewish, about Jewishness, of our history.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they teach you in the Jewish instruction?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't teach us Yiddish, no. He was speaking in Polish, from the Bible, from the beginning of Moses, and Jewish, you know. How it was rather the history, not the Jewish-- yes, everybody laugh at the Jewish classes. And, well, that was that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your Jewish life like at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew that we are Jewish, of course. It was kosher home. And they have separate milk, separate milk. And we have maid, always.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your maid's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name? [INAUDIBLE]? Yes, one was [? Hanka, ?] but before this was [? Yuska. ?] They were very primitive peasant girls, but they live with us. And they were paid very little, like something like 20 zlotys a month. And they had folding bed in the kitchen. And they ate separately. They'd eat in the kitchen. It was-- life wasn't easy at that time. I mean, we'd been middle class. But still, you buy-- you buy butter from peasant woman in the marketplace. It wasn't in packet like this. And milk, you have to go and measure milk to the pot and boil the milk, because it was going-- it wasn't pasteurized or anything like that. And everything, we buy, buy in the market. My mother went to the-- with the maid. And the cooking was starting from morning. Main meal was the lunchtime. I remember my father close his shop and come for a, a lunch. It was tablecloth, white tablecloth, and silver cutlery. We always have our good crockery.

speaker: interviewer
text: Every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every day, because finish school, 2 o'clock-- from 8:00 to 2:00. It wasn't afternoon. And when that was done, lunch. After this, my father lie down to have a little rest, and then opened the shop again, 3 o'clock. And that was this. Every morning we might go to buy fresh bread, fresh rolls. That was baked twice a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you able to buy kosher food, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but I don't think that we did. I don't-- oh, yes, I remember that-- yes, I remember the shochet to kill the chicken. [INAUDIBLE] that we did. That [? means ?] we probably did kosher meal, yeah. But the shop was open all week, Saturday as well. My father wasn't very religious. He was observant. I mean, we went-- we knew about Rosh Hashanah. We knew about Pesach. There was wonderful cutlery and crockery on the attic for Pesach. It was, everything, so beautiful. I-- I don't know why that was only for one week [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Can-- do you remember a festival that you celebrated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, it was big evening. My father was lying, and he got a white, something, shirt, whatever, with-- embroidered with gold. And he was saying, [INAUDIBLE]. And because I was the younger, I asked [HEBREW]. I remember that, and, well, it was a very good evening, a very, very happy time. The whole week, I was looking forward for that. It was beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who was at the Seder?

speaker: interviewee
text: We always have somebody. It was my mother's brother that time. When I was very, very little, was my grandmother, my mother's mother. She lives with us. But that was-- that was not before the war. It was a long time ago [INAUDIBLE]. That was on [POLISH] Street. That's when I was born, yeah. And that was always somebody with us on the Pesach.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you celebrate any other festivals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rosh Hashanah yes, Rosh Hashanah was-- have challah, and, and some cake, and whatever it was. My father go to shul, to temple, and my mother, yes. And I remember Yom Kippur. They were fasting. And I-- as I was little, I tried to fast [INAUDIBLE] [? it was hard. ?] I never could [? stay it ?] all day, but my brother was fasting, and my sister. It was a big day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you-- did you have a Shabbat dinner?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. When my grandmother was alive, that when I was very little, I remember she has her own candlestick. And my mother had candles as well, Friday night. Yes, yes, it was Friday night, but not particularly very celebrating Shabbat. But my mother did pray. I remember how she covered.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that school finished at 2 o'clock.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what would you do in the afternoon every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to a Planty. You know, Cracow was a beautiful, beautiful city, got a park going through the city. All around and through the city, you can go everywhere through the Planty. Planty was a park, was Planty. You've never been in Cracow. Cracow is such a beautiful city, and I got such a good memory. And I went with the maid for a walk, because we didn't have a garden. [? And the ?] courtyard was just concrete. And we went for a-- one hour, afternoon, to walk through the garden, through the Planty. Everyone knows about the Cracow Planty.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet anybody there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I, I remember that sometimes I went with my cousin. I got a cousin, still-- one cousin who is still alive, in Canada, in Toronto. And she was the only child.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rozia-- her name was Weitzenblum, but her name of the-- is Apelowicz now. We still talk to each other on the phone, from time to time. That's the only cousin alive, and she lost everybody, whole family. And then, yes, she got a special nurse, like [? nana. ?] And she was-- they were richer than we, and she had a pram for doll. And she let me, sometimes, to go with the pram and carry-- that was very long time ago. I was a really little girl, from school. Well, that's all, what I remember from my childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish, all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents did speak Yiddish, but only when they want us not to understand. But I didn't know, unfortunately. It was sad for me not to know Yiddish, especially when I come to Australia. I have such a problem with that, because I was working in a-- in a factory. And this Jewish man couldn't understand how a Jewish girl cannot speak Yiddish. But I didn't, and I didn't understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your first experience with anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I couldn't call it first, because it was continuously something there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like that school, that singing, that before Christmas, before-- especially before Easter, and that procession on the street-- you know what is procession? They were walking with the-- with the mother, and with the Jesus with the crosses. And going there, and all everybody was kneeling, and was-- and I didn't. And then I feel that I am different. But that I wouldn't say that were bad to me. They didn't hurt me, anyway. But that hurts me, because I got the feeling that is-- I'm different. And my parents, especially my mother, when she was alive, tried to be-- tried to avoid this. She tried to be similar to the other, to all the friends. And I have, all around me, Catholics, because Poland was Catholic. And we celebrate-- and they come to Easter. They paint the eggs. And my mother let me do it the same, and put in a basket. Then when the girls went to church, I went with them. My mother was very tolerant. And once, I remember, for Christmas she bought a little tree, but my father was very cross. I have to throw it away, [INAUDIBLE]. But I have all-- I didn't really feel that [INAUDIBLE] I am something bad about that, because I was Jewish, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that ever change?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really, not till the war time. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go after you lived at the most recent address before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the war-- before, just before the war, [POLISH]. That's where we are live, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and where did you go after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived there a few months, still, when the German were there. We were ordered to wear the arm band. That was the first, first thing. And it was already embarrassing, and [INAUDIBLE] on the street. And that was so many different orders. Every few weeks was something different that you don't-- you don't allow to go on the street. You have to, not on the foot, but you have to walk here. Then you're not allowed to be after 8 o'clock or 9 o'clock or something like this. We have to be home. So many different ordering every day, every few days And then we went--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you about the orders?

speaker: interviewee
text: What? German, there were German. And actually, not, not directly German-- straightaway, they make a police, or they make somebody Ukrainian. And then they made Jewish policemen. And we know about that. That was on the-- on the wall everywhere, pinned up, [INAUDIBLE] what we have to do, what we are not allowed to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still going to school then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm, and then we have to go out of Cracow. We have to leave Cracow. And then when we were to Tonie.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Tonie?

speaker: interviewee
text: What was-- was Tonie? That was a country. [INAUDIBLE] it's not a country, more like a farm, where we live in, like a farm house with the peasant.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the horse and, and buggy, how you call it, yes? We took with us all our possession, whatever. We have pots, and pans, and, and, and, you know, that quilt or whatever, pillows. The pillows were very big pillows. And everything was put on that, and we went to Tonie. And we live out there. And then I was already maybe 16, 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, when you packed for Tonie, do you remember what you packed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't really. I don't remember. I know that the, the carriage was full of our-- all our possession. Whatever we could take, we [? got. ?] I remember it was-- we had some jewelry. [? But ?] my sister took care of it, you see. She was much older. And she was-- that time, she was like my mother, because I lost mother, as I told you, when I was 11.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who went to Tonie?

speaker: interviewee
text: When my brother come from army, because it was war already, and it was my sister, my father, his brother-- younger brother, who was never married. That was my father's sister, which took us. And a cousin, it was lot of us, whole group of, of Jewish people, and we live up there. It was nice. It was like holiday.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take to get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: How long we been there?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, how long did it take to go from Cracow to Tonie?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was very-- not very close. Well, it was-- that time, it was far, you know? When we go, like, from here to the city, that was already very, very far, that we, we took with us sandwiches and [INAUDIBLE]. That was a [INAUDIBLE], but it wasn't really far. [INAUDIBLE] maybe in an hour or even less than this. The Cracow [INAUDIBLE] everything was smaller than it's here. Everything here is big.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about the house in Tonie where you lived.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was a-- that was a [? house ?] which was really like a-- and I don't know how you call that, not a country. Less than a country, that poor peasant, peasant house. It didn't have roof. It was straw, and not a floor, just clay. But we have our quilt. [INAUDIBLE] quilt, [INAUDIBLE] that was a big, [INAUDIBLE], and big pillows. And my father didn't work, and it was like a holiday. It was-- remind me of the holiday when I was very little, [INAUDIBLE] and we have many people around us, all [? Poles ?] around us. And music was there, and dancing, because nobody was working.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say working, did your father work before you went to Tonie?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, before? Yes, she did, at her shop. But when it was all this, all this come to stop, and we have to leave everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the shop like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before, before the war it was like a grocery shop. It got everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that near the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Near the [? main-- ?] where we live-- yes, it was in corner. It was [POLISH]-- no, when I was born, it was [POLISH]. And [POLISH] was the other street, and it was corner of [POLISH] and [POLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what it was called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't have any name. I think just a surname on it. [INAUDIBLE], a surname, I think-- that wasn't any name of that shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you work in the shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, in that place, in the first beginning, I was very young. No, I didn't work it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in Tonie. And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that is, that is-- Tonie is after the-- that, the beginning of the war. And that's what you asked me before. That was before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure. Tell me what your-- a day was like in Tonie. Describe to me your day.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a beautiful day. That was summer, as I remember. And I opened my eyes. I saw the boys start to look at me, and, and it was one Polish boy. It was-- he was son of the-- how you call it? Master or major of the [POLISH]. [POLISH] is a word I don't know how you say in English. And he was tall, and blond, and blue eyes. And it was not a Jewish family who lived there. There were some Jews who live in, in that county. And that was five brother, and the older ones was Romek, and he was in love with me. And I was top of the world that time. That was most best time in my life, I might say.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And a war was there. And-- but I didn't know much about that. That was a few months only. I don't know how many.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe I was already 17, because '39 was still-- 16 and 1/2, nearly 17. And I was very strong girl, and I was flirt. And then I got flowers, and I got-- it was beautiful. And we [INAUDIBLE] ordered to-- we have to leave this and go much further to the country, not so close to the [INAUDIBLE] or ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: First time we heard that ghetto is starting to be built, ghetto, I didn't know. What is ghetto? But my sister already knew, and she decided, instead of going to the country, we should go to ghetto, be with Jews. When was the first time you heard about the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was, well, after when we went Tonie, I think eight months or something like this. And then we find out that we have to go. It was just order.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what year this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Year.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was '41 or something like that, '41 I believe [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you in Tonie for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe eight months is all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your Jewish life like in Tonie?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Well, we [? used to ?] have a good time [? nothing, ?] like, like you do on holiday in country. I went-- that first time, they taught me how to swim. It was a little river up there. And I went for the river, and we had-- [INAUDIBLE]. And that Polish boy that thought I was-- [POLISH] was his name. He was a real peasant. And when come that harvest, he was-- with the blade, he was cutting this wheat. It was wheat, and corn, and everything. And that Jewish boy was so handsome. He was flirt, and he was gorgeous. And even my sister was laughing. Always, they fight about me. [LAUGHS] That was terrific time, yeah. But it was very short time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened after Tonie? Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We go to ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cracow-- it was in [POLISH], that again suburb of Cracow, [? right? ?] And it was already frightening for me. It was very frightening, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you you had to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you you needed to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not, not directly to me, but we knew that come order to go to ghetto. But I don't know who was the one. It was order, don't know, not directly to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was [? the decision. ?] Some people did go further. And we found out they all perished. [? They went to ?] [? Auschwitz, ?] [INAUDIBLE]. But we went to ghetto. It was a-- oh, you want to know about ghetto? How did you get from Tonie to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, with the horse and buggy, and something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who went?

speaker: interviewee
text: All family, some of the family-- for instance, [? Romek ?] [POLISH], got four other brothers, they went somewhere to-- [? further. ?] They didn't go to ghetto. Only he comes to ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Romek, ?] one of the boys-- there was five of them. And he came to ghetto because of me. That's what he told me. And a few of the-- one of my uncle went. And my father didn't want to go, I think, in the beginning. But he went to ghetto as well. It was such a time, indecision. You didn't know what, what to do with themselves. People didn't know. At this time, they didn't know. But my sister was insisting that we go with the others to Jewish, with Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: How old was I at the time? Still 17 or 18, 17, 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my sister was 10 years older than I. Yes, [INAUDIBLE]. My brother was seven years older. I was [? the younger. ?] Actually, I, I shouldn't be born, because my, my mother was going for an abortion when she was pregnant with me. That, I heard from my sister. She told me the story. They didn't want-- it was such a big difference. And they just-- but somebody told her, no, have a baby. It will be happy. And I was born. But I knew, all the time, [INAUDIBLE]. My father was disappointed. He wanted to have a boy. And when I was young, I have short hair, always. And I was riding on a horse. He wanted a boy. But in the end, he find out that he got more naches from the girl than the boy. My, my brother was a naughty boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do, that he was naughty?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many wrong thing, many wrong thing-- my mother was always upset. My mother was crying. In [POLISH], when we lived, before he went to army, to join the army-- well, he-- maybe the reason, he was small like me. [INAUDIBLE] little bit-- my sister was taller. He was red-head. And he got all Polish friends, my brother. And he, he had a complex that he was Jewish. And he did many wrong thing, want he shouldn't have done.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Kuba. ?] [? Jacob. ?] That-- [? Kuba, ?] we call him [? Kuba. ?] Same as my uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: The name of your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister, [? Yanka, ?] Joanna, Joanna-- my sister was everything to me, really. She brought me up, and, and when my mother died, it was such a tragedy for me. It was such a trauma. I was all by myself in [POLISH]. And, and that was the time-- the worst time of my life, maybe, when my mother died. But that was before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So talking about Cracow ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the first time you heard about the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know how it looks, ghetto. I know, in Tonie, that we are going to ghetto. I didn't know what that mean. But when we arrive, I know that was a wall, brick wall. And it was a entrance, like a big, big entrance there. And were Germans standing on this entrance. And we were located in some [POLISH] Street, I think, in [POLISH]. It was huge, big, very big, big house. Many, many family lives up there. [INAUDIBLE] was already one couple with two girls. He was making chocolate, that man. And he was still doing this during the-- ghetto was like a little, little town in the beginning, in the beginning. The shops were open, and bakery was-- normal life, only very squeezed. We were very squeezed. We were very uncomfortable. But And it was hard to get food. My father used to go out of ghetto, took of this arm band, and brought some flowers and whatever, for, for us to have it. And it was-- that's when I met my husband, in ghetto. Ghetto got a [? coffee land, ?] coffee, cafeteria, and was dancing and music.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll end the first tape there now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
